generations.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    max_a_idx = max(range(n), key=lambda i: a[i])
    max_b_idx = max(range(n), key=lambda i: b[i])
    
    result = []
    for i in range(n):
        # Can i beat max_a player?
        beat_max_a = (i == max_a_idx) or (b[i] > b[max_a_idx])
        # Can i beat max_b player?
        beat_max_b = (i == max_b_idx) or (a[i] > a[max_b_idx])
        if beat_max_a or beat_max_b:
            result.append('1')
        else:
            result.append('0')
    print(''.join(result))

Actually Y is unbeatable iff Y has the maximum a OR... no. Y is unbeatable iff for all Z: NOT(a[Z]>a[Y] OR b[Z]>b[Y]) = a[Z]<a[Y] AND b[Z]<b[Y]. This means Y dominates everyone = Y has max_a AND max_b simultaneously, which is impossible unless n=1.

So every player can be beaten by someone? Not necessarily - the player with max_a can only be beaten on map 2, so someone with higher b can beat them.

Let me re-examine: X can't win iff ∃Y dominating X such that Y is unbeatable by anyone else. Y unbeatable by anyone else means Y has max_a AND max_b - only possible if n=1.

So for n>1, everyone can win? But example 1 shows that's wrong.

The player with max_a and max_b (if same player) beats everyone. Others can't win.

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    max_a = max(a)
    max_b = max(b)
    res = []
    for i in range(n):
        dominators = [(a[j]>a[i] and b[j]>b[i]) for j in range(n)]
        can_win = True
        for j in range(n):
            if a[j]>a[i] and b[j]>b[i]:
                beatable = any((a[k]>a[j] or b[k]>b[j]) for k in range(n) if k!=j and k!=i)
                if not beatable:
                    can_win = False
                    break
        res.append('1' if can_win else '0')
    print(''.join(res))

Actually, a player P can win if and only if no player dominates P on both maps... No, in example 1, player 3 is dominated by player 4 only on both maps, but player 3 still can't win? Wait player 3: a=3, b=3. Player 4: a=4, b=4. Player 3 can't beat player 4. But player 2 can't beat player 4 either. So player 3 can't win because player 4 always beats player 3.

So: P can win iff P can beat every player that no one else can eliminate for P. More precisely, P can win iff we can order eliminations such that P beats everyone.

I think the key insight is: P can win if and only if for every player Q that dominates P (beats P on both maps), Q can be beaten by some player R (on at least one map), and R can be eliminated after Q.

Actually simpler: P can win iff the set of players that dominate P can all be eliminated by other players before facing P. A player Q can be eliminated if there exists any player R ≠ P such that R beats Q on at least one map... but that's almost always true unless Q has max on both maps.

The player with max_a AND max_b (if same player) can never be beaten - only they can win (plus potentially others).

Let me think: P can win iff every player Q with a_Q > a_P AND b_Q > b_P can be beaten by some other player (not P). Since any player not having both global maxima can be beaten by someone.

So P can win iff: for all Q dominating P, Q is not the "unbeatable" player. The only unbeatable player is the one with max_a and max_b simultaneously... but those might be different players.

P can win iff: there's no player Q such that Q dominates P AND Q cannot be beaten by anyone (Q has max_a AND max_b). But also we need a chain...

After more thought: P can win iff every player that beats P on both maps can themselves be beaten by someone on at least one map. Since we can arrange order freely, we eliminate all such threats first.

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    res = []
    for i in range(n):
        can_win = True
        for j in range(n):
            if i != j and a[j] > a[i] and b[j] > b[i]:
                # j dominates i, can j be beaten by someone other than i?
                beatable = any(k != i and k != j and (a[k] > a[j] or b[k] > b[j]) for k in range(n))
                if not beatable:
                    can_win = False
                    break
        res.append('1' if can_win else '0')
    print(''.join(res))

bugs.description: The algorithm incorrectly assumes a player can win if they can beat either the max‑a player on map b or the max‑b player on map a. This condition is not sufficient; there are cases where a player beats one of those maxima but still cannot become champion because they cannot beat the remaining players. Example: n=3, a=[3,2,1], b=[1,3,2] – player 3 (index 2) satisfies the condition but cannot win the tournament.

The algorithm incorrectly assumes a player can win if they can beat either the max‑a player on map b or the max‑b player on map a. This condition is not sufficient; there are cases where a player beats one of those maxima but still cannot become champion because they cannot beat the remaining players. Example: n=3, a=[3,2,1], b=[1,3,2] – player 3 (index 2) satisfies the condition but cannot win the tournament.

The algorithm incorrectly assumes a player can win if they can beat either the player with maximum strength on map A (using map B) or the player with maximum strength on map B (using map A). This condition is not sufficient; a player may beat one of the two maximal players on one map but still be unable to eliminate the other maximal player, making it impossible for them to win the tournament. For example, with players (a,b): (5,1), (4,5), (3,4), the third player is marked as winning by the code but cannot actually win.

The algorithm incorrectly assumes a player can win if they can beat either the player with maximum strength on map A (using map B) or the player with maximum strength on map B (using map A). This condition is not sufficient; a player may beat one of the two maximal players on one map but still be unable to eliminate the other maximal player, making it impossible for them to win the tournament. For example, with players (a,b): (5,1), (4,5), (3,4), the third player is marked as winning by the code but cannot actually win.

fixes.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    max_a_idx = max(range(n), key=lambda i: a[i])
    max_b_idx = max(range(n), key=lambda i: b[i])
    
    result = []
    for i in range(n):
        beat_max_a = (i == max_a_idx) or (b[i] > b[max_a_idx])
        beat_max_b = (i == max_b_idx) or (a[i] > a[max_b_idx])
        if beat_max_a and beat_max_b:
            result.append('1')
        else:
            result.append('0')
    print(''.join(result))

pipelines.final_code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    max_a_idx = max(range(n), key=lambda i: a[i])
    max_b_idx = max(range(n), key=lambda i: b[i])
    
    result = []
    for i in range(n):
        beat_max_a = (i == max_a_idx) or (b[i] > b[max_a_idx])
        beat_max_b = (i == max_b_idx) or (a[i] > a[max_b_idx])
        if beat_max_a and beat_max_b:
            result.append('1')
        else:
            result.append('0')
    print(''.join(result))

bugs.description: The algorithm is incorrect: it only checks one level of domination and does not correctly determine if a player can be eliminated via a chain of defeats. For example, with players (a,b) = (3,1), (2,3), (1,2) the code outputs '111' but the correct answer is '110'.
The solution has O(n^3) time complexity due to three nested loops over all players, which will time out for n up to 1e5.

The algorithm is incorrect: it only checks one level of domination and does not correctly determine if a player can be eliminated via a chain of defeats. For example, with players (a,b) = (3,1), (2,3), (1,2) the code outputs '111' but the correct answer is '110'.
The solution has O(n^3) time complexity due to three nested loops over all players, which will time out for n up to 1e5.

fixes.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    max_a = max(a)
    max_b = max(b)
    
    # Find if there's a player with both max_a and max_b
    # Such a player dominates everyone and can't be beaten
    res = []
    for i in range(n):
        can_win = True
        for j in range(n):
            if j == i:
                continue
            if a[j] > a[i] and b[j] > b[i]:
                # j dominates i; can j be beaten by anyone other than i?
                if a[j] == max_a and b[j] == max_b:
                    can_win = False
                    break
        res.append('1' if can_win else '0')
    print(''.join(res))

pipelines.final_code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    max_a = max(a)
    max_b = max(b)
    
    # Find if there's a player with both max_a and max_b
    # Such a player dominates everyone and can't be beaten
    res = []
    for i in range(n):
        can_win = True
        for j in range(n):
            if j == i:
                continue
            if a[j] > a[i] and b[j] > b[i]:
                # j dominates i; can j be beaten by anyone other than i?
                if a[j] == max_a and b[j] == max_b:
                    can_win = False
                    break
        res.append('1' if can_win else '0')
    print(''.join(res))

Actually, a player P can win if and only if no player dominates P on both maps... No, in example 1, player 3 is dominated by player 4 only on both maps, but player 3 still can't win? Wait player 3: a=3, b=3. Player 4: a=4, b=4. Player 3 can't beat player 4. But player 2 can't beat player 4 either. So player 3 can't win because player 4 always beats player 3.

So: P can win iff P can beat every player that no one else can eliminate for P. More precisely, P can win iff we can order eliminations such that P beats everyone.

I think the key insight is: P can win if and only if for every player Q that dominates P (beats P on both maps), Q can be beaten by some player R (on at least one map), and R can be eliminated after Q.

Actually simpler: P can win iff the set of players that dominate P can all be eliminated by other players before facing P. A player Q can be eliminated if there exists any player R ≠ P such that R beats Q on at least one map... but that's almost always true unless Q has max on both maps.

The player with max_a AND max_b (if same player) can never be beaten - only they can win (plus potentially others).

Let me think: P can win iff every player Q with a_Q > a_P AND b_Q > b_P can be beaten by some other player (not P). Since any player not having both global maxima can be beaten by someone.

So P can win iff: for all Q dominating P, Q is not the "unbeatable" player. The only unbeatable player is the one with max_a and max_b simultaneously... but those might be different players.

P can win iff: there's no player Q such that Q dominates P AND Q cannot be beaten by anyone (Q has max_a AND max_b). But also we need a chain...

After more thought: P can win iff every player that beats P on both maps can themselves be beaten by someone on at least one map. Since we can arrange order freely, we eliminate all such threats first.

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    b = list(map(int, input().split()))
    res = []
    for i in range(n):
        can_win = True
        for j in range(n):
            if i != j and a[j] > a[i] and b[j] > b[i]:
                # j dominates i, can j be beaten by someone other than i?
                beatable = any(k != i and k != j and (a[k] > a[j] or b[k] > b[j]) for k in range(n))
                if not beatable:
                    can_win = False
                    break
        res.append('1' if can_win else '0')
    print(''.join(res))
